exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.title: A: Write one word in each gap
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: In spite of there (1) ...... a great deal of interest in sustainable design, there (2) ...... to be a considerable gap between what studios promise and what they actually deliver.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite + there, so the correct answer is "being, seems".

wrongOptions.1: The bare infinitive (1) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the subject and any time expressions before you conjugate — they decide tense and agreement.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (1) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: There are plenty (3) ...... examples of designers who have insisted (4) ...... expressing their creativity freely and have faced professional setbacks for it.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Plenty of examples; insist on expressing creativity.

wrongOptions.3: The bare infinitive (3) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conjugate (3) to match the subject and the time clues in the sentence.

items.sentence: If you are going to produce controversial work, you've got to be prepared (5) ...... the reaction it may provoke. That reaction may not always be positive. You may be stared (6) ......, criticised (7) ...... expressing yourself so unconventionally, or even ridiculed.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Prepared for the reaction; stared at; criticised for expressing yourself.

wrongOptions.5: The bare infinitive (5) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Choose the future form that matches whether the sentence predicts, plans, or arranges.

items.sentence: On the other hand, you may be complimented (8) ...... your originality and vision. Context is very important in the art world. At a commercial gallery, for (9) ......, you should probably be more anxious (10) ...... appeal to buyers than focused purely on artistic expression.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Complimented on originality; for example; anxious to appeal to buyers.

wrongOptions.8: The bare infinitive (8) is not conjugated — adjust tense and person to fit the sentence.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check subject–verb agreement, tense, and whether the verb form matches the time clues in the sentence.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Time words like already, yet, since, and for are strong signals for present perfect.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals and semi-modals express ability, obligation, permission, or possibility — match the nuance in the sentence.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.title: B: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals when this is necessary.

items.sentence: The director hired a professional (STYLE) ...... to create a coherent visual identity for the entire exhibition.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is person who styles, so the correct answer is "stylist".

wrongOptions.style: The root STYLE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "stylist".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of STYLE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: STYLE → stylist: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "stylist" is the word-formation answer from the root STYLE in this context.

items.sentence: There is a remarkable (SIMILAR) ...... between the early works of these two celebrated artists.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from SIMILAR, so the correct answer is "similarity".

wrongOptions.similar: The root SIMILAR cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "similarity".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of SIMILAR that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: SIMILAR → similarity: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "similarity" is the word-formation answer from the root SIMILAR in this context.

items.sentence: You should lower your (EXPECT) ...... a little; not every piece sells the first time it is shown.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is plural noun from EXPECT, so the correct answer is "expectations".

wrongOptions.expect: The root EXPECT cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "expectations".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of EXPECT that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: EXPECT → expectations: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "expectations" is the word-formation answer from the root EXPECT in this context.

items.sentence: Let me know when you reach a (DECIDE) ...... about which art school you plan to apply to.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from DECIDE, so the correct answer is "decision".

wrongOptions.decide: The root DECIDE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "decision".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of DECIDE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: DECIDE → decision: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "decision" is the word-formation answer from the root DECIDE in this context.

items.sentence: Critics have greeted the new installation with great (ENTHUSE) .......

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from ENTHUSE, so the correct answer is "enthusiasm".

wrongOptions.enthuse: The root ENTHUSE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "enthusiasm".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of ENTHUSE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: ENTHUSE → enthusiasm: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "enthusiasm" is the word-formation answer from the root ENTHUSE in this context.

items.sentence: The studio has increased its (PRODUCE) ...... of handmade ceramic pieces to keep up with rising demand.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is noun from PRODUCE, so the correct answer is "production".

wrongOptions.produce: The root PRODUCE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "production".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of PRODUCE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: PRODUCE → production: Change the word class to a noun with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "production" is the word-formation answer from the root PRODUCE in this context.

items.sentence: That sculpture is absolutely (BEAUTY) ......!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is adjective from BEAUTY, so the correct answer is "beautiful".

wrongOptions.beauty: The root BEAUTY cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "beautiful".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of BEAUTY that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: BEAUTY → beautiful: Change the word class to an adjective with the right suffix.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "beautiful" is the word-formation answer from the root BEAUTY in this context.

items.sentence: These ceramic pieces are designed to appeal to serious collectors and casual buyers (LIKE) .......

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is both groups, so the correct answer is "alike".

wrongOptions.like: The root LIKE cannot fill the gap alone — you need a derived form like "alike".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the suffix and word class — the gap needs the form of LIKE that fits grammatically, not just the root.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Identify the word class needed (noun, adjective, adverb) before choosing the suffix from the root in capitals.
explanationContent.wordFormation: LIKE → alike: Apply the correct suffix or word form from the root.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "alike" is the word-formation answer from the root LIKE in this context.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.title: C: Complete the second sentence using the word given
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
You really should get yourself a new camera. **about**
It ...... a new camera.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is long overdue, so the correct answer is "is about time you got".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is about time you got", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I hate it when you wear that ridiculous oversized beret! **wish**
I ...... wear that ridiculous oversized beret!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present wish, so the correct answer is "wish you would not".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "wish you would not", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Natalie regrets wearing such formal clothes to the studio open day. **had**
Natalie ...... such formal clothes to the studio open day.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past regret, so the correct answer is "wishes she had not worn".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "wishes she had not worn", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I bought the vintage jacket even though it was second-hand. **despite**
I bought the vintage jacket ...... second-hand.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite + gerund, so the correct answer is "despite it being".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "despite it being", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
It's a real shame I can't get to the exhibition this weekend! **only**
If ...... get to the exhibition this weekend!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wistful if only, so the correct answer is "only I could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "only I could", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
In spite of my pleading with her all evening, Sophie refused to lend me her new scarf. **though**
Sophie refused to lend me her new scarf ...... all evening.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is concession, so the correct answer is "even though I begged her".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "even though I begged her", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I don't want you to adopt exactly the same creative style as mine. **rather**
I ...... adopt exactly the same creative style as mine.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is preference about another person, so the correct answer is "would rather you did not".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "would rather you did not", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I can't go to the gallery opening as I have nothing suitable to wear. **something**
I'd go to the gallery opening ...... suitable to wear!

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is second conditional, so the correct answer is "if I had something".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "if I had something", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

items.sentence:
Despite being a retiree, Elena still pushes herself to create bold new work. **fact**
In spite ...... is a retiree, Elena still pushes herself to create bold new work.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is despite the fact, so the correct answer is "of the fact that she".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "of the fact that she", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: I'm just going to pop **C** into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.
items.answer: C – into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.
acceptedAnswers: C – into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Pop into → pop into that new art supply shop.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: Read the beginning and ending together — only one ending forms a natural, grammatical sentence.
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: I'm just going to pop into that new art supply shop for a couple of minutes.

wrongOptions.F: Ending F ("out of these old brushes, so my tutor is going to order me some professional ones.") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".
wrongOptions.A: Ending A ("on that artist's smock you've got hanging near the studio door.") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".
wrongOptions.G: Ending G ("on as a legitimate art form, but it has!") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".
wrongOptions.D: Ending D ("up and the photographer took our individual portraits one by one.") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".
wrongOptions.B: Ending B ("off my paint-stained apron, but I think it's stuck to my shirt underneath!") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".
wrongOptions.E: Ending E ("up for the end-of-year art school celebration party!") does not collocate naturally with "I'm just going to pop".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not force ending F ("out of these old brushes, so my tutor is going to order me some professional ones.") — only C completes "I'm just going to pop" naturally.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Say the full sentence aloud — if it sounds unnatural, try a different ending letter.
